mnemonics.title: Normal Equations Memory
mnemonics.content: NEtA: NEt = A^T — Remember: Normal Equations A^T A x = A^T b

mnemonics.title: QR Solution Path
mnemonics.content: QR Solve: Quick Reply → Q^T b then back-substitute R x = Q^T b

mnemonics.title: SVD Solution
mnemonics.content: VSV: Very Simple Vector — x = V Σ⁺ U^T b (Reciprocal singular values on diagonal, transpose U)

acronyms.usage: Matrix factorization A = UΣV^T used for solving ill-conditioned least squares and computing pseudoinverse
Standard least squares minimization of (b - Ax)ᵀ(b - Ax)
Least squares with weights: minimize (b - Ax)ᵀW(b - Ax)
The minimum value of ||b - Ax||² at the optimal solution

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a 3D plane (column space) and a point b above it. The shortest distance is perpendicular to the plane. Drop a perpendicular from b to the plane — that's the projection, and the line segment is the residual.
Draw the residual vector r pointing 'down' from b to the plane, and show r is perpendicular to all vectors lying in the plane (column space). This is like a right triangle with the plane.
Think of Q as rotating the coordinate system and R as scaling/reshaping. After QR, solving Rx = Q^T b is like solving a triangular system — like going 'up' a ladder step by step.

analogies.analogy: Like finding the closest point on a wall to a picture hanging in 3D space — you can't put it on the nail, so you find the spot on the wall closest to where the picture wants to be.
Like trying to stand on the lowest point of a curved valley — you find where the slope is zero (gradient = 0), which gives you the valley floor (minimum error).
The residual is like the 'miss distance' — if you try to hit a target with arrows but always miss, the residual measures how far each arrow landed from the bullseye, squared and summed.
